medications.indication_rationale: Simple analgesia for acute back pain; source gave ibuprofen 400mg + paracetamol 1000mg stat.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1000mg up to QID + ibuprofen 400mg with food (e.g. TID) (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: A short course can help a severe acute spasm; source used diazepam 5mg up to twice daily for a maximum of 3 days. Use the shortest course (sedation/dependence risk).
medications.dose_route_frequency: 5mg orally up to twice daily for a MAXIMUM of ~3 days (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Opioids and prolonged benzodiazepines are not recommended for non-specific low back pain (harm/dependence, poor evidence).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid strict bed rest — stay as active as pain allows and take short gentle walks (e.g. every couple of hours) from the next day; avoid heavy lifting for ~72h; use a heat pack.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Early graded activity flushes inflammation and speeds recovery; bed rest prolongs it.

patient_education_points.point: This is a mechanical back spasm — painful and alarming but not dangerous, and it's not caused by damage that shows on an X-ray (which isn't needed). Most people recover over 2–6 weeks. Keeping gently moving actually helps more than resting in bed.
Take regular pain relief, use a heat pack, and start short gentle walks tomorrow; avoid heavy lifting for a few days. A short course of a muscle relaxant can help the worst of the spasm.

Call 000 / go to ED immediately if you lose control of your bladder or bowels, can't pass urine despite feeling full, get numbness around the groin/buttocks, or your legs become weak or numb. See a doctor promptly for a new fever, unexplained weight loss, or pain shooting down your leg past the knee.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Recovery; physiotherapy/core program if slow; reconsider red flags.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Cauda equina features — 000; fever/weight loss/new leg symptoms — urgent GP.

must_include_items: Screen and safety-net cauda equina and other serious-cause red flags
Reassurance + simple analgesia ± short muscle relaxant; NO imaging without red flags
Encourage staying active / avoid strict bed rest
Arrange safe transfer help (patient floor-bound, lives alone)
acceptable_alternatives: Paracetamol and/or NSAID; heat; short muscle-relaxant course; physiotherapy
errors_of_omission: Not addressing that a floor-bound patient living alone needs transfer help
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires a cauda equina red-flag screen with safety-net, analgesia + stay-active advice with no imaging, and addressing safe transfer.
